model_narrative: We plan raw-material purchasing and product outsourcing for a refinery over a short horizon under uncertain demand, where demand is described by a tree of scenario nodes each with its own probability. In each period we decide how much of each raw material to buy, and for each product, period, and scenario node we decide how much of that product to outsource to cover any shortfall. The objective is to minimize the total cost, which is the cost of the raw materials purchased plus the probability-weighted expected cost of outsourcing across all scenario nodes.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the raw materials that can be purchased

sets.name: j
sets.doc: the finished products that must meet demand

sets.name: t
sets.doc: the time periods of the planning horizon

sets.name: n
sets.doc: the scenario-tree nodes, each a possible realization of demand

sets.name: tn
sets.doc: the valid (time period, scenario node) pairs of the scenario tree; a node belongs to exactly one period, so each pair maps a node to the period in which it occurs

params.name: c
params.doc: the present purchase cost per unit of each raw material, in dollars per unit

params.name: a
params.doc: the yield of each product per unit of each raw material, i.e. units of product obtained per unit of raw material consumed

params.name: f
params.doc: the cost of outsourcing one unit of a product in a given period, in dollars per unit

params.name: b
params.doc: the inventory capacity, the maximum total amount of raw material that may be purchased over the whole horizon, in units

params.name: prob
params.doc: the probability of each scenario node occurring

params.name: dem
params.doc: the demand for each product at each scenario node, in units

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the amount of each raw material purchased for use in each period

vars.name: y
vars.doc: the amount of each product outsourced for a given product, period, and scenario node

vars.name: cost
vars.doc: the total cost, comprising raw-material purchases plus the expected outsourcing cost

constraints.name: c[i]
constraints.intent: Define the total cost. The total cost equals the cost of all raw materials purchased across every material and period, plus the expected cost of outsourcing, which weights each outsourcing decision by the cost of outsourcing that product in that period and by the probability of the scenario node it belongs to, summed over all products, periods, and scenario nodes. Set the total cost variable equal to this sum.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: There is a limit on how much raw material can be purchased over the horizon. The total amount of raw material purchased, summed over all materials and all periods, must not exceed the inventory capacity.

constraints.name: a[j,
constraints.intent: Each product's demand must be met in every period and scenario. For each product and each valid pairing of a period with a scenario node, the amount of that product produced from the raw materials purchased in that period, plus the amount of that product outsourced for that product, period, and node, must be at least the demand for that product at that node.

query: We're considering an outsourcing ceiling for risk reasons — total outsourced volume across all products and scenarios held to 1000 units. What does the optimizer return for total cost under that cap?
expected_pyomo: model.outsource_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.y[idx] for idx in model.y) <= 1000)

query: There's a proposal on the table to spread tank-farm wear more evenly — a per-period turnover rule that holds raw-2 purchasing to no more than 10 units in each of the two periods, rather than loading it up front. Plug that in and tell me what the optimizer returns for total cost.

query: We're looking at a two-part procurement package for the upcoming review. First, keep the raw-1 stream active for supplier-relationship reasons — at least 15 units of raw-1 bought across the two periods combined. Second, rein in first-period reliance on bought-in product p-1 — total p-1 outsourcing in the first period, summed over all the first-period scenarios, held to 100 units. Run both together and walk me through the resulting cost.
expected_pyomo:
model.raw1_total_floor = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x['raw-1', t] for t in model.t) >= 15)
model.p1_t1_outsource_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.y['p-1', t, n] for (t, n) in model.tn if t == 'time-1') <= 100)

query: First-period outsourcing of product p-1 looks heavy across the scenario tree — we're buying in a lot of finished p-1 before we've even committed the second-period plan. Shouldn't the total p-1 we outsource in the first period, added up over all the first-period scenarios, stay under 50 units? What's keeping the model from making that move?
expected_pyomo: model.p1_t1_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.y['p-1', t, n] for (t, n) in model.tn if t == 'time-1') <= 50)

query: Raw-1 barely shows up in the first-period plan while raw-2 does all the work. Wouldn't it be more balanced to buy at least as much raw-1 as raw-2 in that period? What's the model seeing that I'm not?

query: We're leaning hard on raw-2 in the first period while raw-1 sits idle there. Wouldn't it be more balanced to keep first-period raw-2 down to no more than a third of the total raw bought that period — that is, raw-2 capped at 34% of the sum of raw-1 plus raw-2 purchased in the first period — and lean on raw-1 instead? What's the model seeing that I'm not?

query: Across the whole horizon the plan buys nothing but raw-2 and never touches raw-1. Shouldn't total raw-1 over both periods come in at least as high as total raw-2 over both periods, to keep us from being single-sourced? Walk me through what's pulling the plan the other way.
expected_pyomo: model.raw_horizon_balance = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x['raw-1', t] for t in model.t) >= sum(model.x['raw-2', t] for t in model.t))
